s: The Victorian gin palace that the Luftwaffe couldn't kill
w: Refurbished in 1885 to its current riot of glazed tiles, carved mahogany and stained-glass snugs, it survived a bomb blast from the Europa Hotel next door. The National Trust owns the building but the pints keep flowing. Still the most ornate pub in Ireland.
_facts: Refurbished 1885 as a Victorian gin palace on Great Victoria Street
Owned by the National Trust, leased to Mitchells & Butlers
Adjacent to the Europa Hotel, one of the most-bombed hotels in Europe
Interior features carved mahogany, gilded ceilings and private snug booths
Grade A listed building

s: 1720 tavern where the United Irishmen plotted revolution
w: Built in 1720 and one of the oldest pubs in Belfast, it served as a meeting point for the Society of United Irishmen in the 1790s. Henry Joy McCracken, leader of the 1798 uprising, is said to have hidden under the counter here when British soldiers came calling. Low ceilings, stone floors.
_facts: Built 14 March 1720, one of Belfast's oldest surviving pubs
Meeting place for the Society of United Irishmen in the 1790s
Henry Joy McCracken, who led the 1798 rebellion in Ulster, reputedly hid under the bar counter
Located at 30-32 Bank Street in the city centre
Listed building

s: The last pub in Belfast still fitted with its Troubles-era cage door
w: The security cage over the front door dates from the Troubles, when every Belfast pub needed protection from bomb attacks. The Sunflower kept it as heritage: you walk through the iron grille to reach the beer garden and live folk music beyond. Craft beers, pizza hatch, no pretension.
_facts: Located at 65 Union Street, Cathedral Quarter
Retains the original iron security cage door from the Troubles era, said to be the last in Belfast
The cage door was a standard anti-bomb security measure for Belfast pubs during the conflict
Now a popular music venue and craft beer pub with outdoor beer garden
Known for traditional Irish music sessions

s: Belfast's last Victorian covered market, built in three phases 1890-96
w: Three hundred traders, live music on Fridays and a farmers' market on Saturdays have revived what was once a slaughterhouse and open-air market. The cast-iron roof and brick walls have stood since 1896. Voted best market in the UK and Ireland three times running. The fish stall smells authentic.
_facts: Last surviving Victorian covered market in Belfast
Built in three phases between 1890 and 1896, commissioned by Belfast Corporation
Previously housed a slaughterhouse and open-air meat market before 1890
Now hosts around 300 traders on Fridays, Saturdays and Sundays
Voted Best Large Indoor Market in the UK multiple times

s: The 'Crum' — Victorian jail that held IRA prisoners, loyalists and murderers alike
w: Designed by Charles Lanyon and opened in 1845, modelled on Pentonville Prison in London. A tunnel ran under the Crumlin Road to the courthouse opposite, carrying prisoners between hearing and cell. Seventeen men were hanged here. Closed in 1996, now a tourist attraction with ghost tours.
_facts: Opened in 1845, designed by Charles Lanyon in Pentonville radial design
Only remaining Victorian-era prison in Northern Ireland since 1996
Underground tunnel linked the prison to the courthouse across Crumlin Road
17 executions carried out here, the last in 1961
Held both republican and loyalist paramilitary prisoners during the Troubles; known as 'the Crum'

s: The dry dock where the Titanic was fitted out before her maiden voyage
w: Completed in 1911 and large enough to hold RMS Titanic, this is where the ship's funnels were fitted and sea trials prepared. At 887 feet long it was the largest dry dock in the world at the time. You can walk the floor and see the original keel blocks still in place. Eerie quiet.
_facts: Completed 1911 at Harland & Wolff's Queen's Island yard
At 887 feet, was the largest dry dock in the world when built
RMS Titanic was fitted out and completed here before her 1912 maiden voyage
Original keel blocks remain on the dock floor
Now a heritage attraction open to visitors alongside the pump house

s: The only surviving ship designed by Titanic's architect, now docked in Belfast
w: Launched 25 April 1911 at Harland & Wolff, she was built as a tender to ferry first-class passengers out to the Olympic and Titanic in Cherbourg harbour. The last White Star Line vessel afloat, she later served as a floating restaurant in Paris before returning home. Designed by Thomas Andrews.
_facts: Launched 25 April 1911 at Harland & Wolff, Belfast
Built as a tender to transfer first- and second-class passengers to RMS Titanic and RMS Olympic at Cherbourg
Only surviving vessel designed by Thomas Andrews, Titanic's chief designer
Last White Star Line ship in existence
Previously served as a floating restaurant in Paris before being restored to Belfast

s: Twin gantry cranes that built the Titanic and still define the Belfast skyline
w: Goliath arrived in 1969, Samson in 1974 — each stands over 96 metres tall and spans 140 metres. They were built to assemble supertankers after Titanic-era shipbuilding had long ended, and are now Grade A listed. On foggy mornings they loom above the rooftops like two steel giants keeping watch.
_facts: Goliath crane built 1969, Samson crane built 1974 at Harland & Wolff's Queen's Island yard
Each stands 96 metres tall with a span of 140 metres
Named after the biblical figures Samson and Goliath
Grade A listed structures and dominant feature of the Belfast skyline
Built to assemble supertankers; now iconic symbols of Belfast's industrial heritage

s: Victorian concert hall where Led Zeppelin debuted 'Stairway to Heaven' in 1971
w: Opened in 1862 as the city's grandest venue, it hosted everything from political rallies to the Ulster Orchestra. Led Zeppelin played their first live performance of 'Stairway to Heaven' here on 5 March 1971. The Mulholland organ (1904) still fills the room. Grade A listed.
_facts: Opened 1862 at 34 Bedford Street, Belfast city centre
Led Zeppelin performed the world live premiere of 'Stairway to Heaven' here on 5 March 1971
The Mulholland Grand Organ (1904) remains a centrepiece of the hall
Grade A listed building hosting concerts, classical recitals and political conferences
Home of the Ulster Orchestra

s: Frank Matcham's 1895 oriental fantasy theatre, bombed twice, still standing
w: Designed by the architect behind the London Coliseum, it opened 23 December 1895. The auditorium — elephant heads, gilded boxes, Moorish arches — is cited as the finest surviving oriental-style theatre in the UK. Bombed by the IRA in 1991 and 1993; each time the baroque excess was painstakingly restored.
_facts: Designed by Frank Matcham and opened 23 December 1895
Interior described by the Theatres Trust as 'probably the best surviving example in the UK of the oriental style applied to theatre architecture'
Bombed by the IRA in 1991 and again in 1993; each time restored
Grade A listed building
Features elephant head decorations, gilded boxes and Moorish arches

s: Belfast's leaning clock tower — built on waterlogged land above the buried River Farset
w: Completed in 1869 to mourn Prince Albert, it began tilting almost immediately. The tower leans about 1.25 metres from true vertical because it was sunk on wooden piles into the reclaimed mudflats of the River Farset. Locals say those who have drunk too much stand straight beside it. 35 metres tall.
_facts: Completed 1869 at Queen's Square, Belfast
Tilts approximately 1.25 metres from vertical due to subsidence on waterlogged ground above the buried River Farset
Built on timber piles driven into reclaimed mudflat
35 metres tall; Grade B+ listed
One of the most recognised landmarks in Belfast city centre

s: Belfast's oldest library — and the last subscription library in all of Ireland
w: Founded in 1788 as the Belfast Society for Promoting Knowledge, it holds the world's largest collection of material on the Northern Ireland Troubles, including government documents, paramilitary leaflets and ephemera. Members still pay an annual subscription. The reading room remains gloriously unchanged.
_facts: Founded 1788 as the Belfast Society for Promoting Knowledge
Oldest library in Belfast and last subscribing library in Northern Ireland
Holds one of the world's largest collections of Troubles-era political ephemera
Located at 17 Donegall Square North
Independent charitable body; members pay an annual subscription

s: A Tudor Revival Catholic church so elaborate Berlioz praised its 'extravagance'
w: Built 1844 and modelled loosely on Henry VII's Chapel at Westminster, the interior is a whirl of fan-vaulted ceilings, twisted columns and red brick. It predates Belfast City Hall by over 60 years. Thomas Carlin, who built it, raised the funds by popular subscription among the city's Irish Catholic community.
_facts: Built 1844, predates Belfast City Hall by over 60 years
Tudor Revival style, modelled in part on Henry VII's Chapel at Westminster Abbey
Interior features elaborate fan-vaulted ceiling and twisted stone columns
Second oldest Catholic church in Belfast in continuous use
Funded by public subscription from Belfast's Catholic community

s: A cathedral 130 years in the making — and still not finished
w: Construction began in 1899 and the nave was consecrated in 1904, but the building has been amended and extended in almost every decade since. It is unusual in serving two separate Anglican dioceses simultaneously. The spire of hope — a luminous steel needle — was added only in 2007 for Belfast's City of Culture bid.
_facts: Construction began 1899, nave consecrated 1904, building extensions continued into the 21st century
Unusually serves two separate Church of Ireland dioceses: Down & Dromore and Connor
Romanesque Revival style by Thomas Drew and WH Lynn
The 'Spire of Hope' steel needle added in 2007
Lord Carson of Duncairn, father of Northern Ireland, is buried in the nave

s: 1774 poorhouse built by public subscription — Belfast's oldest intact public building
w: Founded by the Belfast Charitable Institution and opened in 1774, it housed the destitute and elderly of an industrialising town. The elegant five-bay Palladian facade and octagonal lantern tower were designed by Robert Joy. It survived redevelopment to become Belfast's oldest intact public building. Grade A listed.
_facts: Opened 1774, designed by Robert Joy for the Belfast Charitable Institution
Built as a poorhouse through public subscription from Belfast's merchant community
Palladian facade with distinctive octagonal lantern tower
Grade A listed; considered Belfast's oldest intact public building
Still partly in use as residential accommodation

s: Redemptorist church off the Falls Road where secret peace talks began in 1988
w: Built in the Romanesque style and consecrated in 1911, the monastery became a discreet meeting point during the Troubles. Father Alec Reid facilitated secret back-channel talks between Sinn Fein's Gerry Adams and SDLP leader John Hume here in 1988 — a process that ultimately led to the Good Friday Agreement.
_facts: Built by the Redemptorists and consecrated 1911; large Romanesque basilica off the Falls Road
Father Alec Reid of Clonard facilitated secret talks between Gerry Adams (Sinn Fein) and John Hume (SDLP) in 1988
These talks formed the foundation of the peace process that led to the 1998 Good Friday Agreement
Father Reid was photographed administering last rites to a soldier during the 1988 corporals killings nearby
Still an active Redemptorist parish

s: The iron and concrete divide between nationalist and unionist West Belfast
w: First erected as temporary barriers in 1969, this stretch on Cupar Way grew to five metres tall over the decades. The gates were locked nightly for years; they opened 24 hours from 2013. Visitors leave messages on the painted steel. Over 100 peace walls remain in Belfast.
_facts: First peace lines erected 1969 as 'temporary' barriers; some grew to over five metres tall
The Cupar Way wall separates nationalist Falls Road from unionist Shankill Road areas
Gates in the wall were locked nightly for decades; unlocked 24 hours from 2013
Over 100 peace walls and barriers remain across Belfast as of 2024
International visitors and locals have covered sections with painted messages and flags

s: A church for dockland sailors where the pulpit is a ship's prow and the font a ship's binnacle
w: Built in 1857 to serve the merchant seamen and dock workers of Sailortown, the interior was fitted with maritime fittings as homage to the congregation: the pulpit is shaped as a ship's bow, the font is a ship's binnacle, the offering plates are lifeboats. The clock on the tower shows ship's time. Corporation Street.
_facts: Built 1857 to serve merchant sailors and dock workers in Belfast's Sailortown district
Pulpit is designed as the bow of a ship; the baptismal font is a ship's binnacle
Offering plates are in the shape of miniature lifeboats
The exterior tower clock displays ship's time (24-hour format)
Grade B1 listed building; still an active Presbyterian congregation

s: One of the oldest surviving curvilinear iron glasshouses in the world
w: Begun in 1839 and completed in 1852, one of the earliest curvilinear iron glasshouses in existence — predating Kew's Palm House by several years in parts. Designed by Richard Turner of Dublin, who later co-designed Kew's. Free entry; tropical plants, old iron, Belfast rain on the glass.
_facts: Construction began 1839, wings completed by 1852, making it among the earliest curvilinear iron glasshouses extant
Designed by Richard Turner of Dublin, who later co-designed the Palm House at Kew Gardens
Predates the Kew Palm House (completed 1848) in part
Grade A listed; located in the Belfast Botanic Gardens, University Quarter
Still in use as a tropical and temperate plant house

s: One of the old 'entries' — the medieval alleyways threading through Belfast's original street plan
w: Belfast's old commercial centre was threaded with narrow passages called 'entries' linking main streets to merchants' yards. Pottinger's Entry — named for Sir Henry Pottinger, first governor of Hong Kong — is one of the few survivors. The Old Stock Exchange once opened onto it. Tiny pubs still lurk here.
_facts: One of several historic 'entries' (narrow alleyways) threading the original street plan of Belfast's commercial centre
Named for Sir Henry Pottinger, first Governor of Hong Kong
The entries were used as service lanes between main streets and merchants' backyards from the 17th century
The Old Stock Exchange of Belfast formerly had an entrance onto Pottinger's Entry
One of a small number of original entries still accessible in Belfast city centre
